chunk_summary: Guidance on selecting and interpreting skin prick tests (SPT) and specific IgE (sIgE) for diagnosing IgE-mediated food allergies, emphasizing clinical context, test limitations, and resource-adapted strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of SPT and sIgE in confirming IgE-mediated food allergies.
Identify scenarios where SPT or sIgE testing is contraindicated or not feasible.
Learn resource-adapted strategies for managing suspected IgE-mediated allergies in limited settings.
Differentiate between sensitization and clinical allergy based on test results and history.
key_facts: SPT and sIgE should only be used when pretest probability of IgE-mediated allergy is moderate to high.
A positive SPT or sIgE indicates sensitization, not clinical allergy, unless it aligns with a compatible reaction history.
Negative SPT or sIgE results lower the probability of IgE-mediated allergy but do not predict severity risk.
SPT is preferred when experienced operators and quality extracts are available, as results are immediate.
sIgE is recommended when SPT is contraindicated, such as in cases of severe eczema or inability to stop antihistamines.

For children with clear, reproducible immediate reactions but no access to SPT or sIgE, manage as likely IgE-mediated with avoidance, an emergency action plan, and referral for confirmatory testing or supervised oral food challenge (OFC).
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Testing should confirm a well-founded clinical suspicion rather than create one. Start with a history that defines timing, organ systems, dose, and reproducibility; then choose targeted tests only when pretest probability is moderate to high. (Source: Rational Use of Allergy Testing, IAP Action Plan 2026)
document_key_topics: Role of SPT and sIgE in food allergy diagnosis
Limitations and interpretation of allergy testing
Clinical differentiation between sensitization and allergy
